summary: I was born in Taipei, Taiwan in 1996 before my family immigrated to New York City. Although I left Taipei at a young age, I have visited Taiwan every two years since I was 10 to see my mother's family. Each visit made me feel more like a tourist, as I became more distant from the language and culture of Taipei over time.
cues: visited every two years since age 10

summary: Taiwan is where I was born and where my mother's family still lives. Growing up in New York City, I visited Taiwan regularly every couple of years since I was 10, but over time I felt more disconnected from its language and culture. Despite this distance, Taiwan remains an important part of my heritage and identity.
cues: visited every two years since age 10

summary: My family immigrated to New York City when I was a child, and I grew up mainly in Brooklyn and Staten Island. Growing up in New York City exposed me to a diverse environment, although I often felt different from my mostly Caucasian friends. It was in New York City where I learned English for the first time when I started public school.
cues: grew up in Brooklyn and Staten Island

summary: I spent a significant part of my childhood growing up in Brooklyn, New York City. In Brooklyn, I had mostly Caucasian friends and often felt like the Asian one in the group, which made it hard to connect with them. This experience shaped how I viewed my cultural identity growing up in the United States.
cues: feeling like the Asian in the group

summary: Alongside Brooklyn, Staten Island was another place where I grew up in New York City. Living in Staten Island contributed to my diverse upbringing in the United States. It was part of the environment where I balanced my family's Mandarin and Burmese languages with the English spoken outside.

After growing up in New York City, I went on to study at the University of Minnesota. Attending this university was an important step in my education and personal growth. It allowed me to further explore my identity and experiences as an immigrant in the United States.

Mandarin Chinese was the language my parents spoke to me and my brother at home when we lived in New York City. Although I started school without knowing any English, Mandarin was a key part of my early life and cultural connection. Over time, as I became more fluent in English, my Mandarin skills suffered, but my parents continued to speak it to us to keep the tradition alive.

My parents also spoke Burmese to me and my brother at home, which helped us become trilingual alongside Mandarin and English. My mother used Burmese kinship terms like 'thami' for daughter and 'thatha' for son, which gave me a personal connection to our Burmese heritage. This language was a vital part of maintaining our family's cultural traditions despite living in the United States.

Burma is where my father comes from, and it is part of my family's cultural background. Although I grew up in New York City, my parents' experiences in Burma and Taiwan shaped how they raised me and my brother. The Burmese language and traditions they passed down helped me maintain a connection to Burma despite living far away.

The United States is where my family immigrated to from Taiwan when I was a child, and where I grew up in New York City. Living here exposed me to a diverse culture and allowed me to learn English, which became my dominant language. Although I sometimes felt caught between cultures, I am grateful for the opportunities and experiences the United States has given me.
cues: grew up in New York City
